Trachea midline. Respiratory: CTA bilaterally, no crackles, rales, wheezes, rhonchi. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs or gallops, normal S1, S2, no peripheral edema. Gastrointestinal: Abdomen soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds normal. Genitourinary: No CVA tenderness, No suprapubic tenderness. Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Hospital Course 81-year-old female with past medical history of abnormal uterine bleeding and a fib on anticoagulation, was scheduled for hysterectomy was found to have a low potassium at her PCP prior to scheduled surgery was brought to Valley Hospital Medical Center on 3/23/2026 to treat hypokalemia. Patient surgery was rescheduled for the following week. Upon admission patient hemodynamically stable, saturating well on room air. Potassium elevated 2.4 EKG showed atrial fibrillation. Patient is not on anticoagulation for her A-fib due to severe GI bleeding previously. Patient required multiple doses of IV and oral potassium to replete levels reading of 4.0. PT oT evaluated the patient and determined that patient would benefit from acute rehab following discharge to improve functional deficits. Recommended the patient follow-up with her PCP regarding hypokalemia. On discharge, patient has stable vitals, labs within normal limits, and acceptable physical exam. Patient denies any symptoms at this time. Patient was advised to follow-up with PCP within the next 1-2 weeks. Patient is to return to ED if symptoms worsen or if there are any questions or concerns. All questions and concerns were answered and addressed at bedside. Patient is currently alert and oriented to person, place, time, situation. Risk, benefits and alternatives conveyed. Patient has verbalized understanding of diagnoses, management, treatment, and follow-up instructions. Patient is safe for discharge at this time. Greater than 30 minutes were spent in the examination, evaluation, counseling, and review of medications and discharge plan for this patient. This patient will be discussed with the attending physician. Further recommendations to follow. Cole, an 81-year-old female, requires home health following a recent hospitalization for hypokalemia, acute kidney failure, and hypomagnesemia. Her potassium level was critically low at 2.4 mmol/L, requiring IV repletion and close monitoring. She also has a history of atrial fibrillation on anticoagulation, hypertension, and arthritis, contributing to her medical complexity. Functional limitations affect her ability to perform some activities of daily living independently, and she is at risk for falls due to ambulation deficits, decreased stride length, and reliance on a walker. Physical therapy evaluation indicates she would benefit from skilled therapy to improve mobility and reduce fall risk. Skilled nursing services are required for ongoing monitoring of her potassium and renal status, as well as medication management, while physical therapy will focus on balance training and safe ambulation. Her home is equipped with assistive devices, including a shower chair and wheelchair, but she has limited family support, making professional care essential to ensure safety and prevent functional decline. The combination of recent acute medical issues, ongoing skilled care needs, and functional limitations supports the medical necessity for home health services. Skilled nurse reviewed seizures with patient, explaining triggers, effects on body, and when to seek help. SN provided instruction on medication timing and safety measures during a seizure, walked through steps to protect from injury, and highlighted lifestyle factors such as sleep, stress, and avoiding triggers to reduce recurrence. Patient and caregiver explored tracking seizure patterns to support ongoing management. Skilled nurse reviewed fall risks with patient, explaining how environmental hazards, balance issues, and medication effects contribute to falls. SN walked through safety strategies such as using assistive devices, removing clutter, wearing proper footwear, and arranging furniture for clear pathways. Patient and caregiver explored routines to improve strength and balance, highlighted importance of pacing activities, and provided instruction on when to ask for help to reduce fall risk at home.
